APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This work sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it tackles lexical semantics and lexical substitution, proposes a novel multi-view clustering method (SubstClus), and evaluates against relevant baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus) on standard datasets (CoInCo, PPDB, Gigaword) using appropriate metrics (B-Cubed F-score, GAP). The problem formulation, methods (multi-view NMF, silhouette-based model selection), and evaluation protocol match community expectations for a conference paper and target the right audience of researchers in lexical semantics and representation learning. The paper is positioned correctly with comparisons to prior work and clear relevance to improving lexical substitution systems.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a notable novel combination: applying B-Cubed F-Score to measure how sense inventories match human substitutability judgments and introducing SubstClus, a multi-view clustering method that explicitly optimizes substitutability by combining context substitutability (AddCos), PPDB paraphrase similarity, shared translations, and WordNet synset affinity. This clearly differentiates it from prior PPDBClus work (Cocos & Callison-Burch, 2016) and from crowdsourced inventories like TWSI, and the methods go beyond simple reuse of existing techniques by integrating multiple views via NMF and selecting clusters for substitutability. While not a paradigm shift, the approach is a non-trivial, informative advance that would interest experts in lexical substitution and sense induction.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-organized and follows a logical flow from methodology (multi-view NMF SubstClus) through experiments (B-Cubed F-Score, GAP evaluations) to conclusions, and its main contributions are stated early (proposing B-Cubed evaluation for substitutability and introducing SubstClus). Notation and experimental details (datasets, baselines, hyperparameters) are reported, and the writing quality is described as good. Minor presentation issues remain (e.g., some evaluation nuances such as WSD failure modes and coverage trade-offs are noted but could be presented more clearly), so I rate it as well-written with only small presentation improvements needed.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper evaluates against appropriate and recent baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus/Cocos & Callison-Burch 2016, Syn.VSM, AddCos) and uses the same datasets and metrics (CoInCo subset, B-Cubed F-score; GAP) for comparisons. It acknowledges when prior methods outperform the proposal (e.g., TWSI on nouns) and reports limitations of its WSD/best-fit procedure that affect performance. The authors clearly analyze differences by attributing gains to the multi-view SubstClus components (context substitutability, paraphrase similarity, translations, WordNet) and compare coverage and substitutability against prior automatic clustering, so comparisons are comprehensive and fair.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper provides solid experimental evidence: comparisons to WordNet, TWSI, and PPDBClus using B-Cubed F-Score and GAP, oracle vs best-fit analyses, coverage/threshold tradeoffs (PPDB cutoff), and reports hyperparameters and cluster selection (Silhouette). It also discusses limitations (weaker on nouns, WSD failures). However, the summary does not indicate explicit ablation of the individual multi-view components or release of code/replication artifacts, and evaluation is on a CoInCo subset, leaving minor gaps that prevent an excellent (5) rating.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of producing sense inventories optimized for human substitutability and proposes using B-Cubed F-Score to measure this match, which is directly relevant to lexical substitution and related tasks. Its SubstClus multi-view clustering (context substitutability, paraphrase similarity, translations, WordNet) yields inventories with greater coverage than WordNet and more substitutable clusters than prior automatic PPDB clustering, and using these inventories as filters measurably improves lexical substitution GAP in oracle and best-fit settings. Noted limitations (weaker gains for nouns, WSD best-fit failures, and coverage tradeoffs from PPDB cutoffs) temper the contribution but do not undercut its potential to shift how researchers construct and evaluate task-oriented sense inventories.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims are backed by experiments on a reasonable evaluation suite (CoInCo subset, PPDB XXL, TWSI, baselines WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus, Syn.VSM, AddCos) using appropriate metrics (B-Cubed F and GAP) and a clear method (multi-view NMF with context substitutability, paraphrase similarity, translations, WordNet, and cluster selection via Silhouette). There are no apparent mathematical errors (no complex derivations required) and hyperparameters are reported; the authors also acknowledge limitations (weaker on nouns, best-fit WSD failures, PPDB cutoff tradeoff). Minor concerns remain about missing details on statistical significance testing and reliance on a best-fit WSD that often fails, but these do not overturn the experimental support for the main conclusions.

main_claims: Proposes using B-Cubed F-Score to measure how well a sense inventory matches human substitutability judgments for lexical substitution.

Introduces a novel multi-view clustering method (SubstClus) that clusters PPDB paraphrases into sense inventories optimized for substitutability using multiple views (context substitutability, paraphrase similarity, shared translations, WordNet synsets).

The resulting automatically-generated sense inventories have greater coverage than WordNet and produce more substitutable clusters than previous automatic paraphrase clustering (Cocos and Callison-Burch, 2016).

Applying these SubstClus sense inventories as a filter over existing lexical substitution ranking models (Syn.VSM, AddCos) significantly improves lexical substitution performance (GAP) in oracle and best-fit settings.
_summary.methodology: Generate sense clusters for a target by multi-view nonnegative matrix factorization over representations encoding context substitutability (AddCos scores over sampled sentences), paraphrase-paraphrase similarity (PPDB2.0 scores), shared translations, and WordNet synset affinity, choose cluster count via Silhouette Coefficient, and evaluate substitutability with B-Cubed F-Score and impact on lexical substitution rankings (GAP).
limitations: SubstClus inventories are weakest at improving lexsub rankings for nouns; crowdsourced TWSI outperforms them on nouns.
The simple best-fit WSD method frequently fails to select the correct sense cluster, causing Best-Fit GAP to deviate from Oracle GAP; WSD method is an area for improvement.
Applying a PPDB score cutoff (2.3) reduces noise but also decreases coverage of the resulting sense inventory.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - lexical substitution and sense inventory / paraphrase clustering
